hook: Three-storey Gothic cloister, founded by a queen, raised in barely a year
facts: Founded 1326-27 by Queen Elisenda de Montcada, widow of King Jaume II
Three-storey cloister, often called the largest Gothic cloister in the world
Poor Clare nuns lived enclosed here for nearly 700 years, into recent times
Holds a medicinal herb garden and the 1346 Ferrer Bassa murals in Sant Miquel chapel
w: Queen Elisenda buried her king, then built herself a sanctuary, founded 1326 and raised in barely a year, which is why the place feels of one breath. Three storeys of Gothic arcade ring a garden of medicinal herbs; Poor Clares lived enclosed here almost seven centuries. A widow's quiet revenge on grief.

hook: Bohemian terrace bar hidden in a 1650 palace, funding fringe theatre
facts: Housed in a 1650 palace, listed in the city heritage catalogue since 1979
Reclaimed in 2003 by artist Semolinika Tomic and collaborators from ruin
Bar profits fund the venue's independent and experimental performance programme
Tucked between Santa Caterina market and the Palau de la Musica
w: Behind an unmarked door near the Palau de la Musica, a 1650 palace courtyard runs as a leafy bar, every drink quietly bankrolling the fringe theatre upstairs. Artists found it abandoned and in ruins in 2003 and never left. Ivy, mismatched chairs, a fig tree. The best secret nobody guards.

hook: Walled 1875 textile factory turned overgrown creative compound
facts: Built 1875 as the Gal i Puigsech textile factory by Antoni Vila i Bruguera
Squatted by creatives from 1989, became a city-backed foundation in 1998
Run by designer Javier Mariscal, who preserved the industrial fabric
Only Poblenou factory to keep its original perimeter; hosts a monthly market
w: A textile mill from 1875 stood derelict for nine years until designers and artists moved into the shells in 1989 and let the garden swallow the brick. Now a walled creative compound run by Javier Mariscal, the only Poblenou factory still inside its old perimeter. Industry gone feral, on purpose.

hook: Four Roman columns standing in a medieval courtyard on the city's highest point
facts: Four Corinthian columns, 9 metres tall, from a 1st-century BC temple to Augustus
Stood at the forum of Roman Barcino, atop the 16-metre Mont Taber
Hidden in a courtyard at Carrer del Paradis 10, entry free via MUHBA
Three columns surfaced in the 1880s during Centre Excursionista works; a fourth moved here in 1956
w: Follow a Gothic alley off the cathedral, push through a medieval doorway, and four nine-metre Roman columns rear up in a tiny courtyard, all that survives of the temple to Augustus. A millstone outside marks Mont Taber, the highest point of Barcino. The empire, filed away in a stairwell.

hook: A garden-city hospital of jewelled pavilions linked by secret tunnels
facts: Built 1905-1930 by Lluis Domenech i Montaner, world's largest Art Nouveau site
Pavilions rotated 45 degrees for maximum sunlight; UNESCO listed since 1997
A kilometre of underground tunnels let staff move between wards unseen
Served as a working hospital until 2009, now restored as a cultural complex
w: Domenech i Montaner studied two hundred hospitals, then built one that looks like a fairground of mosaic pavilions, the whole thing pivoted 45 degrees to chase the sun. Below ground, a kilometre of tunnels let doctors cross unseen, keeping the gardens calm for the sick. Medicine as opera.

hook: Civil War gun emplacements crowning the best 360-degree view in the city
facts: Republican anti-aircraft battery on the 262-metre Turo de la Rovira hill
Operational from March 1938 with four Vickers 105mm guns covering the city
Postwar, families built the Els Canons shantytown into the concrete walls
Cleared and interpreted by the city from 2015; a MUHBA heritage site
w: Locals call them bunkers, but these concrete pads up the Turo de la Rovira held four anti-aircraft guns that fired on the planes bombing the city in 1938. After the war, shanties grew into the gun walls; some six hundred people lived here. Now the finest 360 view in town, won at altitude.

hook: A whole 1714 neighbourhood, excavated beneath a Victorian iron market
facts: Cast-iron market hall built 1876 by Josep Fontsere i Mestre
Houses 8,000 sq m of streets and homes razed in 1714 for a Bourbon citadel
Remains found in the 1990s; centre opened on 11 September 2013
Entry to the archaeological site is free
w: Diggers prepping a library under this iron market hit something stranger, the streets and cellars of a neighbourhood the Bourbons forced residents to demolish in 1714 to wedge in a fortress. Eight thousand square metres of it, open free under a Victorian roof. A city made to bury itself.

hook: Civil War bomb shelter dug into the flank of Montjuic by neighbours' own hands
facts: Some 400 metres of tunnels dug into Montjuic's base by Poble-sec residents
Designed for around 2,000 people; only half the planned network was finished
Held an infirmary, children's room, toilets, water fountain and a fireplace
Three entrances on Carrer Nou de la Rambla; visit by MUHBA guided tour only
w: When the bombs came, Poble-sec dug its own way out, boring four hundred metres of tunnel into Montjuic by hand. Inside: an infirmary, a fireplace, a little room for the children. Built for two thousand, finished only halfway before the war ended it. Survival, improvised in rock.

hook: One of the world's oldest magic shops, with its own theatre and museum
facts: Founded in 1881 by illusionist Joaquim Partagas, among the oldest magic shops on earth
Shop at Carrer de la Princesa 11; museum and theatre nearby on Carrer Jonqueres
Partagas chose 1881 because the number reads the same backwards
Museum opened 2011 with 19th-century props, posters and apparatus
w: Joaquim Partagas, a chemist seduced by illusion, opened these doors in 1881, picking the year because it reads the same both ways. The Born shop still sells trick cards and vanishing coins; a nearby museum and theatre keep the Victorian apparatus alive. Conjuring, in continuous performance for over a century.

hook: A two-hectare Mediterranean garden the tourists never find
facts: Laid out 1918 by Nicolau Maria Rubio i Tuduri for industrialist Alfredo Mata
An early work in his signature Mediterranean garden style
Splits into a formal classicist half and a wilder, stream-cut half
Free to enter; among the city's least-visited green spaces
w: A cotton baron bought an old family estate and had Rubio i Tuduri shape it into one of his first Mediterranean gardens, half clipped and classical, half left wild where a stream once ran. Fountains, ponds, shaded benches, and almost nobody, even locals forget it exists. Quiet kept on purpose.

hook: A wave of 325,000 coloured tiles over the ruins of a Dominican monastery
facts: Barcelona's first covered market, opened 1845, rebuilt by Miralles and Tagliabue in 2005
Undulating roof of 325,000 hexagonal tiles in 67 colours by ceramicist Toni Cumella
Built over the demolished Dominican monastery of Santa Caterina
Excavated monastic and earlier remains preserved within the market hall
w: Stand back on Avinguda Francesc Cambo and the roof reads as a spilled fruit stall, 325,000 hexagonal tiles in 67 colours rippling over Barcelona's oldest covered market. Underneath, archaeologists found the Dominican monastery the market replaced, kept in view by the stalls. Groceries over ghosts.

hook: Gaudi's wrought-iron dragon guarding a gate to the Garden of the Hesperides
facts: Gaudi's first commission from patron Eusebi Guell, built 1884-87
Five-metre iron dragon gate forged 1885 by Joan Onos
Dragon evokes Ladon of the Hesperides; its form traces the Serpens constellation
Declared a national monument in 1969
legend: Said to depict Ladon, the dragon slain by Heracles guarding the golden apples
w: Before the cathedral, before the park, Gaudi got his first job from Guell: gatehouses and a fence for the family estate. He answered with a five-metre iron dragon, jaws wide, that supposedly maps the Serpens constellation and guards a recreated Garden of the Hesperides. Mythology, hung on a hinge.
